skill.name: stitch-loop
skill.description: Guides agents through an iterative Stitch website build loop using baton files, project context, and generated page integration.
skill.summary: Iteratively builds Stitch website pages with baton-based handoffs and project documentation updates.
skill.author: google-labs-code

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many critical and high alerts, but review found the Windows SAM, weak crypto, C2, and reconnaissance hits are false positives from markdown examples, design-system wording, hex colors, and localhost instructions. The skill is not malicious, but it does request Bash, Write, Stitch, and Chrome tools and imports generated remote assets into a project, so publication should include a moderate warning.

medium_findings.title: Broad Agent Permissions for Site Modification
medium_findings.description: The skill manifest allows Read, Write, and Bash, then instructs the agent to move generated HTML into site/public and update project documentation. This supports the workflow, but it can overwrite project files if run in the wrong repository or on unreviewed input.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permissions and file modification steps are explicit in the skill. The risk is workflow misuse rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: External Generated Assets Are Integrated Into the Project
medium_findings.description: The instructions tell the agent to retrieve HTML and screenshots from Stitch download URLs and save them into the local project. Generated HTML should be reviewed before deployment because remote generated content can contain unwanted scripts, links, or tracking.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The download and local preview workflow is clearly documented. No evidence shows credential theft or unauthorized exfiltration, so the concern remains moderate.

medium_findings.title: Baton Files Can Steer Autonomous Agent Work
medium_findings.description: The loop asks agents to read next-prompt.md and SITE.md before each iteration, and the example SITE.md contains an AGENT INSTRUCTION block. This is expected for the skill, but untrusted project files could steer the agent toward unintended file edits or commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The baton pattern is semantically confirmed and relies on project-authored instructions. The example instruction is not a malicious override, but it shows why trusted context boundaries matter.

low_findings.title: Static Sensitive and Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM and weak cryptography detections are caused by words such as DESIGN SYSTEM, Desktop, and design-system template text. No evidence found of Windows credential database access or cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown design instructions and template labels, not executable code or credential paths. This strongly supports dismissal of those static alerts.

low_findings.title: C2 Keyword Alerts Are Hex Color False Positives
low_findings.description: The C2 detections map to color values and design copy, including charcoal color notation, not command-and-control infrastructure. No evidence found of remote control endpoints or beaconing logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious C2 tokens are embedded in visual design color text. There is no executable network client or suspicious domain in the cited context.

low_findings.title: Most External Command Alerts Are Markdown Formatting
low_findings.description: Many Ruby or shell backtick findings are inline markdown around filenames, tools, and example commands. The real Bash permission is covered as a medium finding, but the repeated backtick detections are not Ruby execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are markdown documentation and examples, not Ruby source files. Backticks delimit code spans and fences, with one legitimate install command example.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Are Local Workflow Text
low_findings.description: The network and system reconnaissance hits refer to localhost previewing, layout principles, or sitemap items. No evidence found of host discovery, port scanning, or network enumeration logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content describes page preview and website planning. It does not contain scanning commands or instructions to probe external systems.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash and Write Tool Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The manifest grants Bash and Write, enabling command execution and filesystem changes during the website build loop. Users should run it only in a trusted project workspace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tools list explicitly includes Bash and Write. The skill needs them, but they expand the operational risk surface.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Download URL Consumption
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow consumes Stitch download URLs for generated HTML and screenshots. Treat generated assets as untrusted until reviewed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The download URL fields are directly named in the instructions. The source is expected Stitch output, so this is a controlled but real trust boundary.

dangerous_patterns.title: Autonomous Iteration Baton
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill requires updating next-prompt.md before completion, which can keep an automated loop running across repeated agent sessions. Human review should gate unattended operation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The loop continuation step is explicit and central to the skill. It is not malicious, but it can amplify mistakes across iterations.

content.value_statement: Repeated site updates can lose context and visual consistency across agent sessions. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a baton workflow for generating, integrating, and documenting Stitch pages.
actual_capabilities: Reads a next-prompt.md baton file to identify the next page to generate.
Uses Stitch MCP tools to create or reuse a project and generate a screen.
Retrieves generated HTML and screenshots from Stitch output metadata.
Moves generated pages into a site/public structure and updates navigation guidance.
Updates SITE.md and next-prompt.md so the next iteration has clear context.
Supports optional Chrome DevTools visual checks against the Stitch screenshot.
limitations: Requires access to a configured Stitch MCP server.
Assumes the project uses SITE.md, DESIGN.md, next-prompt.md, queue, and site/public conventions.
Generated HTML still needs human review before production deployment.
Autonomous loops can compound mistakes if project context is stale or untrusted.

use_cases.title: Maintain a Multi-Page Marketing Site
use_cases.description: Generate one new page at a time while preserving the sitemap, visual style, and roadmap.

use_cases.title: Prototype a Product Website
use_cases.description: Turn a design system and backlog into a sequence of Stitch-generated pages for review.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Agent Handoffs
use_cases.description: Use baton files to pass the next task, current site state, and design constraints between sessions.

prompt_templates.title: Run the Next Baton
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup with existing project files.
prompt_templates.prompt: Read next-prompt.md, SITE.md, and DESIGN.md. Generate the requested Stitch page, integrate it into site/public, update SITE.md, and write the next baton.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Missing Baton
prompt_templates.scenario: The project has a roadmap but no current baton.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use SITE.md Section 5 to choose the next unfinished page. Create next-prompt.md with the design system block, then generate and integrate the page through Stitch.

prompt_templates.title: Verify a Generated Page
prompt_templates.scenario: Optional visual review with Chrome DevTools available.
prompt_templates.prompt: After generating the Stitch page, start a local preview server, open the new page in Chrome, compare it with the Stitch screenshot, and fix visible integration issues.

prompt_templates.title: Continue an Autonomous Site Loop
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced multi-iteration workflow with human review gates.
prompt_templates.prompt: Complete one full Stitch loop iteration. Record completed sitemap changes, remove consumed roadmap items, and prepare a precise next-prompt.md for the following agent run.

output_examples.input: Generate the contact page from the current baton.
output: A new contact page is staged in the public site folder.
SITE.md marks the contact page as complete and records the updated sitemap.
next-prompt.md now describes the next page with the required design system notes.

output_examples.input: Continue the loop after the backlog item is finished.
output: The completed roadmap item is removed or marked done.
The next highest priority page is selected from the roadmap.
The baton contains a clear page name, visual guidance, and page structure.

output_examples.input: Preview and compare the generated page.
output: A local preview is opened for the generated page.
The rendered page is compared with the Stitch screenshot.
Navigation and asset path issues are fixed before completion.

best_practices: Review SITE.md and DESIGN.md before every iteration so the generated page matches current project intent.
Inspect generated HTML and screenshots before moving pages into a deployable folder.
Keep next-prompt.md small, specific, and aligned with the roadmap.
anti_patterns: Do not run the loop in a repository that contains untrusted baton or project instruction files.
Do not deploy generated Stitch HTML without reviewing links, scripts, and asset paths.
Do not let the loop continue after the sitemap or design system becomes stale.

faq.question: Does this skill require Stitch?
faq.answer: Yes. It is designed around Stitch MCP tools for project creation, screen generation, and asset retrieval.

faq.question: Can it create a site from scratch?
faq.answer: It can help start a structured site, but it works best when SITE.md, DESIGN.md, and next-prompt.md already exist.

faq.question: Why does it use a baton file?
faq.answer: The baton file gives the next agent a single current task with page name, design constraints, and page structure.

faq.question: Is generated HTML automatically safe to publish?
faq.answer: No. Review generated HTML, links, scripts, and assets before publishing or deploying the site.

faq.question: What tools does it expect?
faq.answer: It expects Stitch MCP tools and file access. Bash and Chrome DevTools are used for optional local preview checks.

faq.question: Can humans stay in the loop?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow supports manual review after each generated page and before the next baton is executed.
